title: Trade and International Business: Research & Analysis | CSIS
gist: This is a CSIS topic/section landing page for 'Trade and International Business', not a single article. It is a catalog of links to commentaries, podcasts, and events (USMCA review, EXIM reauthorization, forced-labor tariffs, China and critical minerals). Card is intentionally light; the only mineable item is the recurring theme that China is winning critical-mineral ownership pre-production, which is stated in the page's article teaser.

themes.theme: Page is a topic index, not an article

insights.claim: The CSIS 'Trade and International Business' page is a section landing page aggregating links rather than a single analytical piece; recurring 2026 trade themes it surfaces are USMCA renewal, EXIM reauthorization, USTR Section 103/122 tariff investigations, and critical minerals.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: No standalone thesis to trade; flags which trade-policy catalysts CSIS is tracking into mid-2026.

insights.claim: Chinese firms are acquiring strategic critical-mineral assets worldwide before mines enter production, meaning the West is losing ownership of future supply at the asset stage rather than the output stage.
insights.speaker: Gracelin Baskaran
insights.so_what: Watch for Western state-backed tools to pre-empt Chinese acquisition of pre-production mining assets; bullish for any allied critical-mineral ownership vehicle.
insights.quote: China is winning the race for critical minerals long before mines enter production.
insights.locator: Why the West Keeps Losing Critical Mineral Assets to China
